next_steps: NDA execution to share proprietary pricing calculator
Prospect to record Loom walkthrough of pricing calculator
Follow-up call to review pricing model and scope implementation

jtbd.job: Get accurate quote data into HubSpot so ARR and MRR reporting is trustworthy
jtbd.context: Revenue reporting is broken because line items from DealHub come through incorrectly. Recurring fees appear as one-time, contract terms are wrong. Cannot forecast or report on pipeline with confidence.
jtbd.desired_outcome: ARR rolls up to company correctly across multiple deals, reportable by term and year, using HubSpot native recurring revenue reporting

jtbd.job: Enforce discount approval policies without requiring manual review of every quote
jtbd.context: Every single proposal requires approval because the rules engine in DealHub is unreliable. Conditions produce incorrect results, so nothing passes through without human review.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Rules-based approval workflows where only policy exceptions trigger review. Discount gates by term length, product, and threshold that work correctly.

jtbd.job: Replace DealHub with a HubSpot-native CPQ before the contract expires
jtbd.context: DealHub contract expiring in 5-8 weeks. Trust in the platform is gone. Sales reps are editing PDFs manually because output is unreliable. The team needs to migrate to a working solution within that window.
jtbd.desired_outcome: CPQ that uses native HubSpot objects (quotes, line items, subscriptions) so data flows correctly to NetSuite and HubSpot reporting without a separate integration layer

jtbd.job: Support complex population-based pricing with multiple business lines and multi-currency
jtbd.context: Four business lines, each with multiple products and tiers. Pricing is calculated by municipality population times product tier rate. Canadian customers need separate CAD price books without automatic currency conversion in HubSpot.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Price books and calculated pricing that handle population-based formulas, multi-tier products, and CAD pricing without manual calculation

pains.pain: 90% of quotes require manual correction because line items come through incorrectly from DealHub
pains.trigger: Every quote cycle. Recurring fees display as one-time fees (and vice versa). Contract start and end dates do not come through. Sales reps resort to editing the PDF directly rather than fixing data.
pains.cost: RevOps reviews every quote individually. Revenue reporting is unreliable. Pipeline forecasting is impossible. NetSuite sync is broken because source data is wrong.
pains.urgency_reason: This is the primary driver of the evaluation. Affects every deal, every month, across all 10-15 quoting users.

pains.pain: Cannot trust ARR, MRR, or revenue reporting because quote data is inaccurate
pains.trigger: Any attempt to report on revenue, forecast pipeline, or analyze discounting patterns. The RevOps leader cannot provide confident answers to executives.
pains.cost: Executive team has no reliable revenue visibility. HubSpot recurring revenue reporting feature (Enterprise) is unusable because input data is garbage. Multi-year deal ARR cannot be broken out by year.
pains.urgency_reason: CFO is direct manager and needs reliable revenue data. This pain flows directly to the economic buyer.

pains.pain: Every single proposal requires manual approval because discount rules are unreliable
pains.trigger: DealHub approval conditions produce incorrect results. Items tagged as recurring come through as one-time, so term-based discount approvals fire incorrectly or not at all.
pains.cost: Three approvers review every proposal. No quote passes through without manual oversight. Bottleneck on deal velocity for 100-150 proposals per month.
pains.urgency_reason: Prospect explicitly said this is not sustainable. Approval burden scales linearly with quote volume.

pains.pain: Renewals are broken because contract term start and end dates do not sync from DealHub
pains.trigger: Every renewal cycle. Cannot determine which contracts are up for renewal or what the renewal terms should be.
pains.cost: 20-30 renewals per month at risk of manual error. Cannot build automated renewal workflows. Subscription management is blocked.
pains.urgency_reason: Renewals are a stated massive pain point. Directly impacts revenue retention.

pains.pain: Sales reps bypass the quoting tool entirely and edit PDFs manually
pains.trigger: Reps have lost trust in DealHub output. When quotes are wrong, they take the path of least resistance: edit the PDF and send it out.
pains.cost: Quote data never enters HubSpot correctly. No audit trail. Pricing and discounting are uncontrolled. The CPQ tool is providing zero value.
pains.urgency_reason: Symptom of the root cause (DealHub data accuracy) but compounds every other pain.

pains.pain: NetSuite integration is broken because DealHub sends incorrect data downstream
pains.trigger: Every closed deal that syncs to NetSuite. Line items, billing frequencies, and terms arrive incorrectly in the ERP.
pains.cost: Finance team must manually correct records in NetSuite. Invoicing may not match quotes. Reconciliation burden on the finance team.
pains.urgency_reason: Mentioned but not deeply explored. The NetSuite person is 'not happy' per the prospect.

pains.pain: DealHub created a half-built custom object (deal hub subscriptions) that adds CRM complexity
pains.trigger: DealHub cannot work with native HubSpot subscriptions, so a custom object was built as a workaround. It remains incomplete.
pains.cost: Technical debt in the HubSpot instance. Prevents using native HubSpot subscription and contract features. Prospect wants to remove it.
pains.urgency_reason: Cleanup item rather than active blocker, but reflects accumulated frustration with DealHub's HubSpot integration approach.

workflow_reality.current_process: Quotes are built in DealHub by 10-15 sales reps. Output is unreliable: line items, billing frequencies, and contract terms come through incorrectly. Reps edit PDFs directly rather than fixing data in DealHub. Every proposal requires manual approval from one of three approvers because the rules engine cannot be trusted. The RevOps leader reviews quotes one at a time to assess data accuracy. Closed deals sync to NetSuite with incorrect data, requiring manual correction by the finance team.
tools_in_use: HubSpot Sales Hub Enterprise (CRM, all Enterprise products)
Excel spreadsheet (pricing calculator with lookup tables, source of truth for pricing logic)
PDF editing (manual workaround when DealHub output is wrong)
brittleness_indicators: 90% of quotes need manual correction after generation
Sales reps bypass the tool entirely and edit PDFs
Every proposal requires approval because rules cannot be trusted
Half-built custom object (DealHub subscriptions) from failed workaround
RevOps leader reviews quotes one at a time rather than in aggregate
Revenue reporting is described as 'useless' and 'garbage' due to data quality
Pricing logic lives in an Excel calculator, not in the CPQ tool

evaluated.name: DealHub
evaluated.what_they_liked: Intended to handle complex pricing and approval workflows. Presumably chosen for enterprise CPQ capabilities.
evaluated.why_they_are_leaving: Data integrity failure. Line items come through incorrectly (recurring vs. one-time fees swapped). Contract terms (start/end dates) do not sync. Rules engine produces unreliable results. Cannot work with native HubSpot subscriptions, requiring a custom object workaround. Sales team has lost all trust in the output.
evaluated.contract_status: Expiring in 5-8 weeks. Prospect extended but is actively evaluating replacements.

current_workaround.description: DealHub generates quotes, but reps edit the PDFs manually. RevOps reviews every quote individually. Pricing logic lives in an Excel calculator with lookup tables. NetSuite data is corrected manually by the finance team.
current_workaround.adequacy: Non-functional. The prospect described the situation as 'jacked' and 'not sustainable.' Zero trust across sales and finance teams.

status_quo_tools.name: Excel pricing calculator
status_quo_tools.role: Source of truth for pricing logic. Contains formulas for population-based pricing, tier calculations, and lookup tables for all four business lines.
status_quo_tools.notes: This is what the prospect trusts. The CPQ tool is supposed to replicate this logic but fails.

status_quo_tools.name: HubSpot native quoting
status_quo_tools.role: Not currently used for quoting but prospect wants to use native HubSpot objects (quotes, line items, subscriptions) rather than external tools or custom objects.
status_quo_tools.notes: Prospect is excited about HubSpot Contracts (upcoming GA ~September 2026) and wants a CPQ that is compatible.

quotes.quote: Nothing is coming in as data. And then when it goes to NetSuite, nothing is correct in NetSuite.
quotes.usage_notes: Clean, quotable articulation of the cascade effect: bad CPQ data corrupts both CRM and ERP. Useful for content about data accuracy and integration reliability.

quotes.quote: I can't rely on revenue reporting. I can't rely on pipeline reporting. I can't forecast correctly. I can't get insight into the line items and discounting correctly.
quotes.usage_notes: Four consecutive 'I can't' statements. Powerful stacking effect for content about the real cost of inaccurate quoting. Maps directly to RevOps persona pain.

quotes.quote: Every single proposal is having to get approved because we just can't trust the rules. There are no proposals that don't need an approval process. And I'm not sure that that's sustainable for all time.
quotes.usage_notes: Defines the approval bottleneck problem precisely. 100% approval rate driven by distrust, not policy. Strong for content about smart approval workflows vs. blanket approvals.

quotes.quote: They're going in and editing the PDF.
quotes.usage_notes: Six words that capture total CPQ failure. When reps edit the PDF, the tool is providing negative value. Strong hook for content about CPQ adoption and trust.

quotes.quote: The DealHub is so hard to use and is so unreliable that people are just smacking around the line items however they feel like it to get something out.
quotes.usage_notes: Vivid language ('smacking around the line items'). Captures how tool distrust leads to process drift and data chaos. Excellent for competitive DealHub content.

quotes.quote: I can't do that right now because I can't trust the data. It's not right.
quotes.usage_notes: Said in context of wanting to use HubSpot subscriptions and recurring revenue reporting. Shows how bad CPQ data blocks adoption of other HubSpot features. Good for HubSpot ecosystem content.

quotes.quote: Renewals are a massive pain point right now.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct statement but lacks specificity on its own. Pair with the context that contract terms (start/end dates) are not coming through, which is the root cause.

quotes.quote: I want to use the HubSpot quotes, the HubSpot line items.
quotes.usage_notes: Clear articulation of the desire for native HubSpot objects over custom objects or external tools. Validates Quotivity's HubSpot-native positioning.

quotes.quote: There is a half built custom object in our system right now called deal hub subscriptions because DealHub can't work with subscriptions. I would like to rip that thing out.
quotes.usage_notes: Concrete example of DealHub's incomplete HubSpot integration creating technical debt. The emotional language ('rip that thing out') shows accumulated frustration. Strong for competitive positioning.

value_language: reported on in terms of term

deviant_terms.meaning: Broken, unreliable, producing wrong output. Used to describe the overall state of DealHub quoting data.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used twice. Clearly an established descriptor for the current state.

deviant_terms.meaning: Sales reps manually editing line item data however they want to force a quote out, bypassing any intended process or controls.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used once, but vividly captures the total process breakdown.

deviant_terms.meaning: A sympathetic salesperson who volunteers to pilot a new tool during the transition. Internal term for early adopters in a change management process.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used once. Indicates the prospect has done tool rollouts before and has a methodology.

deviant_terms.meaning: The gradual degradation of process compliance when the tool is too hard to use or unreliable. Reps find workarounds that compound data quality issues.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used once, but as a diagnosis of the root problem. Shows RevOps sophistication.

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect is actively replacing DealHub due to data integrity failures and incomplete HubSpot integration. Contract expiring in 5-8 weeks.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: When your CPQ breaks HubSpot data: why mid-market teams replace DealHub with HubSpot-native quoting

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect needs population-based calculated pricing, multi-tier products, multi-currency price books, and product dependencies, all inside HubSpot.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to handle formula-based pricing, tiered products, and multi-currency quoting inside HubSpot

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect requires tiered discount approvals (term-based, product-based, threshold-based) but current tool produces unreliable results, forcing 100% manual approval.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Building discount approval workflows in HubSpot that sales reps actually follow (and finance actually trusts)

keyword_candidates.keyword: HubSpot ARR MRR reporting from quotes
keyword_candidates.source: Prospect cannot report on ARR/MRR because quote data is inaccurate. Wants ARR to roll up by company across deals, broken out by year for multi-year contracts.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why your HubSpot ARR reporting is wrong (and how to fix the quoting problem upstream)

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect needs accurate quote data to flow from HubSpot to NetSuite. Current DealHub setup sends incorrect data downstream, requiring manual NetSuite corrections.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Getting clean quote data from HubSpot to NetSuite: why your CPQ is the weakest link

keyword_candidates.source: Renewals described as 'massive pain point.' Contract start/end dates not syncing. 20-30 renewals per month with no automation possible due to data quality.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why your HubSpot renewal process breaks down at 20+ renewals per month (and how to fix it with accurate quote data)

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect needs separate CAD price books for Canadian municipalities without automatic currency conversion in HubSpot. Conversion handled only in NetSuite.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Managing multi-currency pricing in HubSpot without automatic rate translation

keyword_candidates.source: Population-based pricing calculated by formula (population x tier rate). Currently lives in Excel calculator with lookup tables. Needs to be replicated in CPQ.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Replacing your Excel pricing calculator with formula-based quoting inside HubSpot
